feedback.text: From the perspective of an addictive researcher and EU citizen, I welcome in principle this initiative on consumer protection in the digital sphere. In particular, adolescents should be protected as much as possible from the dangers of certain digital applications (in particular social media, gaming and gambling). Solutions for the protection of minors and consumers must always be designed individually and practically; theoretical and empirical research findings should be used as a basis. However, some generic statements can be made that affect all platforms. For example, the business models of tech companies typically aim to retain consumers on a permanent basis through specific manipulative design elements and increase their residence time accordingly in a sustainable manner. In other words: It is always a question of keeping people in play and/or on the platform through often very non-transparent processes and techniques. On the one hand, achieving the objective increases the likelihood of direct (e.g. during play) or indirect (promotional/affiliate marketing) money spending, as well as of phenomena reminiscent of addictive experiences and behaviours. On the other hand, and even more importantly, digital traces will be left. These data can rightly be described as 21st century gold, as they prove essential from an entrepreneurial perspective for product optimisation, tailor-made advertising or other personalised outreach processes. My impulse is here: These data could be used not only in the economic interest of businesses but also in the interests of consumer protection, for example to better reflect the positive and negative effects of digital applications and to derive evidence-based recommendations for action to optimise consumer protection. So far, however, these data have only been publicised on a case-by-case basis. My ideas are therefore: Access for (provider)-independent research to the datasets of the game or platform operators; (2) development of a research infrastructure with rules-based award of research projects; (3) translating the empirical evidence gathered into practice and thus a consumer protection-oriented, fair) development of digital markets. Finally, there is a need to promote media literacy across the board, including media literacy in schools (regardless of the age bracket). It is imperative for policymakers to fulfil this behavioural prevention objective with life and to guarantee implementation. Hence: (4) Dedicated levies on gaming or platform operators as a means of ensuring the financing of this project.
